title: The Root-Crown Where Archives Descend

features.name: the-first-kept-thing
features.desc-on-touch: You find the earliest file the archive remembered to save: a pressed flower from the first survey, its label half-faded. The archive learned to keep by watching stone.

features.name: descending-channels
features.desc-on-touch: Root pathways spiral downward, each one older than the one above. You're reading the tree's annual records in reverse.

features.name: stone-memory
features.desc-on-touch: A marking the archive can no longer explain: a carved spiral in the soil, or the tree's memory of learning to hold. Canonical but unexplained.

features.name: the-blind-survey
features.desc-on-touch: A field recording of two voices mapping territory they cannot see. The archive argues whether this is archive or art: the catalogue says both.

features.name: precursor-forms
features.desc-on-touch: The archive keeps things that preceded itself—works created before recording, now proven canonical by their gravity. The stone remembers the form before the archive could name it.

inscription: the first thing the archive learned to keep was stone; the second was the tree's refusal to forget

file.name: First_Survey_Pressed_Flora_1891.txt
file.note: Catalogue entry lists eleven specimens. Only one remains. Archive notes the others with equal attention.

exits.label: deeper still: where root and stone become indistinguishable
the direction memory climbs when it remembers it has weight
